speaker: interviewer
text: [? Goldberg ?]. Are there any other memories of-- either from Buchenwald, particularly your brother, who you haven't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, Bergen-Belsen, and particularly of your brother, Albert, who you haven't talked about much, at least in Bergen-Belsen--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or memories from Trobitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trobitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, one of the reasons that I didn't speak too much about my brother Al in Bergen-Belsen is because we did not see each other that much while there. As I said, he was with my brother in the men's section, and I was with my mother in the women's section. We did get glimpses of each other now and then, particularly when we were standing on appell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do recall one incident where my brother was given an apple by a soldier who was on guard. I mean, there were always guards surrounding us. And this must have been a particularly long day of standing on appell. And this was a young guard, a young man, who gave my brother an apple. Now, he was jeopardizing his own life by doing so. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother, I remember, sharing that apple into I don't know how many little pieces. And he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was just an incident that that will always be with us. And it just goes to show that despite the horror and the horrendous treatment that we all received by the-- the Germans, that one must not generalize. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It goes to show that there are exceptions. And here was this one young man who was really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] endangering his own life by giving this one apple. He just probably felt the need to do something good and to help in his own way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It-- it-- again, I think something like that gave us a little bit of hope, and-- and it just helped us along. Another very important incident which I had forgotten to bring out was the fact that I had mentioned earlier that we were in Bergen-Belsen actually because we were to be exchanged for a group of Germans in Palestine. The time had come. I believe it was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sometime in-- in '45, may have been [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] February or March of '45, or maybe it was earlier. A few hundred of us were taken out of our section of Bergen-Belsen in camp and put into another area of camp, because it was time for us to be exchanged for a group in Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We knew it was going to happen. We thought, we hoped, we prayed it was going to happen. And we were, of course, overjoyed that the time had come. And we were then in this section, another section, and we were told to get our belongings together. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they would prepare us psychologically and so on and so forth to be exchanged and moved to Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we got to that area of the camp, we were all standing and names were called off. Our name was Blumenthal, started with a B. The commandant started calling the names. He went through the A's. He went through the B's, the C's, and alphabet was completed, and our name was not on the list. We thought maybe at the very end, there-- he maybe made a mistake and left names out. It was not on the list. My father-- we were, at that point, all together. The four of us were together. My father [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] walked up ahead and knew very well that this is not the thing to do. You don't speak first to a commandant, to the Gestapo. You wait to be spoken to. But he just needed to let them know that the-- that the Blumenthals were supposed to be on this list, and that maybe a mistake was made, that our-- our name was not called off. Perhaps there was another list. And the Gestapo, the SS, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stepped forwards and slapped and hit my father and called him terrible names and told him to get back to where he were. And so this was the list, and he had no right speaking up. And if you're not on the list, you're going back to where you came from, and that was that. He was abused. He was kicked. He was slapped. And all this in the presence of his family and everyone else there. We then had to return to Bergen-Belsen, to our section. And that had to be the most disappointing time of our entire stay at Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Out of the 200 and some, 50 were turned back, and we were among the 50. But the other 200 or so did go to Palestine. They were exchanged. And those were the fortunate few. And we were sent back. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Other than that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could look it up. It had-- it-- I believe it was in the spring, and we were still in-- in fairly good health, fairly good condition. So it-- it must have been in '44, somewhere in '44, because by '45 things were absolutely-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just impossible. And we could hardly walk. We could hardly stand on our own. So it-- it had to be in '44. And in fact, a cousin of ours was-- was among those that-- but nevertheless, the fact that we were allowed to go to Bergen-Belsen rather than Auschwitz, because we were in that group to be exchanged, saved our lives. And we're forever grateful to my aunt, who made those arrangements in Palestine. She's-- it's now Israel, and I just saw her a few weeks ago, and she's doing just fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you told the story about the apple, did Albert somehow get pieces of the apple to you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot recall the complete incident, but I do know that it happened. I remember it vividly. In fact, when I speak to my brother Al about it, he doesn't even remember it. He does not remember it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there-- mind-- a mind does play tricks on-- on-- on-- on people. We-- there's certain things that I recall vividly that my mother doesn't remember. And as you have seen earlier, my mother remembers things, and-- and by right, she-- she should, being that she was older and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it-- it's-- it was just very comforting to know that a young man like that was not so completely influenced and taken in by the Nazis. And it-- it-- again, it helped us along in a very, very difficult time-- actually, the-- the-- the worst of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else that you want to recall from Trobitz before we move on to Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just remember that it was springtime when we arrived in Trobitz. And although I was preoccupied with the welfare of my burned leg and-- it was very pretty. It was a farm village, and with old cobblestone streets and farmhouses, quaint farmhouses, and pretty greenery, grass, trees in bloom, flowers. And it must have been a beautiful time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but just I don't think I gave myself the chance to really enjoy it as much. I-- I couldn't, because there was still an awful lot of heartache and misery surrounding us, and-- and I was in constant pain and couldn't walk. And-- but it was enormous gratitude that we survived. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- despite the fact that my father didn't make it, I do feel that my-- my games of superstition that I-- was such a part of my life in Bergen-Belsen was not in vain. I do feel that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I won in that game, because my father did see us liberated, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I-- I see his presence in our children, in our grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I have very mixed feelings about all of this, that that has happened-- very mixed feelings. There-- there are feelings within me of-- of anger. And, of course, there are feelings of gratitude. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anger because how could something so incredible, something so horrendous have happened? And how could people do something like this to one another? Animals don't do this to one another. How could people, so-called intelligent people, allow this to happen? And how could this have been allowed to have happened altogether? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where was the man upstairs? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And yet, I know that people are doing it to themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am grateful that I survived, healthy in mind and body, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I have a wonderful, kind, understanding, and generous, loving husband, and that together we were allowed to perpetuate our heritage with a wonderful family. And we have three wonderful children-- David, Susan, and Michael, who are happily married, David to Lisa, Susan to Rob, and Michael to Rachel. And we have five beautiful grandchildren-- Arielle and Joshua, Gavi, Dalia, and Yo'av. And Michael and Rachel and the family are now in Israel. And there, too, again, are mixed feelings with that. We-- we miss them terribly. We love them dearly. And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they gave up much here-- material things, only material things. But we're very, very, very proud of them that they took it upon themselves to-- to fulfill their inner feelings and-- and hope and to live and-- to give up all that they gave up here to live and fulfill their dream in Israel. And there is no doubt that if we had an Israel at that time, many, many more of us would have survived. There was not a place in this whole world that would take us in. And if we had an Israel then, it would have been altogether different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously, you have a wonderful family to be proud of. Let's go back to Bussum, Holland to see how you got from where you were in 1945 after the war to where you are now. What happened to you in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on this truck, and we first stopped in Leipzig, I remember, in Germany. We had various stops, actually, before we arrived in Holland, obviously, too, because you couldn't do that all in one stretch. In Leipzig, I remember we were sitting at a-- oh, a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a long table. We must have been in-- in some hotel type of a place. Not a hotel-- where soldiers are taken care of. And it was a long table, and it was summertime. It was June. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were all given beer, and I remember enjoying that glass of beer. That was in-- in Leipzig. Then we headed on towards Maastricht, which was in Holland. And there we were put up in a cloister. And my mother had a terrible toothache. And she-- there, instead of going to a dentist and having a filling done, they pulled eight of her teeth. And that was the best way that they could manage that. From there, we-- of course, we were fed along the way. From the time we left Trobitz, there was, thank god, never a problem with food again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We, of course, still had our fears. We didn't know what the future would bring. And it was particularly difficult for my mother. We had absolutely nothing. We didn't even have a country. We were considered stateless, [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We finally arrived in Amsterdam, and there we were taken to the Joodse Invalide. And there most of us lived and also in sort of a communal situation. But it was fine. We had plenty to eat. We were given some clothing by the Jewish communities there. And we learned to smile again. We learned to live and appreciate life in its normal state. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was while we were in this Joodse Invalide decisions had to be made. What would be-- what should we do? Where should we go? Fortunately, there was a Jewish organization, Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] people from a Jewish organization who got together and established a Youth Aliyah home. There were various homes, I'm sure. But this was a Youth Aliyah home that was to be established in Bussum, just about an hour or so south of Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr. and Mrs. Birnbaum, very dear friends of ours who were in camp with us in Westerbork, as well as in Bergen-Belsen, and their six children, who, by the way, all survived Bergen-Belsen-- parents and six children. Three of their six children had polio in Bergen-Belsen and survived. They were in quarantine but miraculously survived. My dear friend Susie was one of them, who is now in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was even paralyzed and here without medication, without even anything physically to fall back on-- no strength, no food, no nothing. They survived. That is a true miracle. That's when we know, yes, there is a man upstairs. We can be angry with Him, though, and can't help but-- but doing that once in a while. I had major conversations with the man upstairs. And I-- somehow I always come away feeling all right. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It was decided that my brother and I would go to Bussum and live in this Youth Aliyah home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother decided to stay in Amsterdam. She [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eventually moved in-- or my-- actually, it was my aunt, my father's sister, who was in hiding in Holland, came forth, and we found one another. And this Aunt Rosie, who also had most of our belongings in hiding with her-- and that's why we were able to save so many documents and personal items was through her-- this Aunt Rosie moved in with my mother in Amsterdam. My mother decided to study masseuse, manicure, and pedicure. This had to be done in Dutch. She never knew Dutch, and she had to learn all of these tissues and muscles and-- and whatnot in Dutch. She's a very, very industrious lady, my mother, and of course did very well and got her diploma or degree. And with it, she eventually was able to get a bicycle. And on her bicycle, she went to her clients and was able to make a living with her new profession as a manicurist, pedicurist, and masseuse. My brother Al and I, we moved to Bussum, as I had said. And we were in this Youth Aliyah home with the Birnbaums, who were in charge of this home where some 25 or so children lived. Most of these children came from camps without their parents. They were orphans. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were in this home in preparation, actually, to go to Palestine. And we were taught Hebrew in the home, and it was a-- an Orthodox upbringing at that time. And our secular studies were in a Montessori school in Bussum. Montessori-- I don't know if you're familiar with the Montessori system. It was an ideal situation for us, children who have had no education whatsoever prior. Here I was, 11 years old, starting in Dutch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never had spoken Dutch and I didn't know Dutch, and we started in Dutch in the Montessori school. We were in a large area, all of us in one big room with a few teachers, and we all learned at our own pace. It worked out well. And we lived very comfortably in this home in Bussum. And it was actually a wonderful life, except that we missed not being with my mother, and I got very homesick many, many times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother did come to visit us once a week. And the following week, my brother and I would visit her over the weekend in-- or just for the day, for Sunday, in Amsterdam. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We became very close with the children in this home and got a very thorough Jewish Hebrew education. And as I said, it-- we were there in preparation to go to Palestine, but our transport was to be an illegal one. It was 1947, '46, '47, and our transport was to be one similar to that of the No adults were allowed to go along. It was only for children. And my mother, again, she feared-- feared for our lives and for our safety. She then decided to make arrangements for us to come to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had to get an affidavit again, and-- and our visa and all these papers had to be put in order. And as my mother made the preparations, she also checked back to Rotterdam to see what had happened to our initial papers from 1939 that we already had intact. Our passage was booked already in '39. It was already paid for. Fortunately and miraculously, the records were intact. And our tickets were on record as having been paid for, and we were allowed to use those tickets to come to the United States. We had to pay a little additional, even though we were only with three instead of our four, but it was a big help financially. And now to get an affidavit, that was a little bit more difficult. Some of our relatives here in the United States were unable to do it. There were others who couldn't or wouldn't do it, give it to us. But then that part was very disappointing, because as members of this family knew how industrious and how wonderful and how hardworking my mother was, and we would never be a burden on them. But for some reason, they didn't want to take the responsibility. But through my Aunt Rosie, my father's sister who lived with my mother, we-- my Aunt Rosie's mother-in-law, [? Oma ?] Wolf, came to visit one day. And when she heard that we were looking, my mother was looking for an affidavit, she said, not to worry. Her son would supply the affidavit. And this is a person who we had never met, and he lived in Malden, Massachusetts. His name was Arnold Wolf. And sure enough, within a few weeks time we had the affidavit. And with it, of course, he needed to vouch for us, that we wouldn't be a burden to the government here, and $600 had to be supplied or given to-- by him and given to us or put in a bank for us to use in case we needed it. Everything was in order, and we left Holland in April of '48 aboard the and the Holland America line. And it was really a wonderful trip, and we arrived in Hoboken on April 23, 1948. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] April 23 is a very memorable day in my-- in our lives. It was on April 23, exactly April 23, 1945, that we were liberated by the Russians. It was April 23, 1948 that we arrived in this wonderful country of America. And it was also April 23, 1979 that I gave my first talk [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on my experiences in the camps as a child. And I know that arriving-- before we arrived in-- in the United States aboard, we were told the night before that if we wanted to see the Statue of Liberty the next morning that we should be on board deck early, early in the morning. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were all, believe me, every one of us was on board. And it was just the most magnificent sight to have seen the Statue of Liberty approaching-- as we approached it. And to this day, when I cross that Verrazano Bridge I strain to see the statue. It has enormous meaning for us. And I just have a-- a wonderful, beautiful feeling inside whenever I see it, and it is just enormously meaningful.
